topic: An employee discovers the company is being sold
raw_output:
[story 1]

Elias smoothed down his already impeccable tie, the silk feeling suddenly suffocating against his throat. He was supposed to be finalizing the Q3 projections, but his eyes kept drifting to the sleek, unfamiliar branding plastered on the wall near the executive elevator. A whispered conversation, too low for anyone else to catch, had snagged his attention—a murmur about an impending acquisition. His palms began to sweat, a clammy film spreading across his fingertips as he tried to focus on the spreadsheet. Every glance felt like an interrogation; he worried his posture was too stiff, his breathing too shallow. He took a sharp, unnecessary sip of cold coffee, the bitterness a welcome distraction from the unsettling knowledge that the structure he had built his career upon was about to be dismantled.

[story 2]

"Are you sure you want to look at this, Sarah?" Mark asked, his voice pitched slightly too high, a nervous tremor underlying the professional veneer. Sarah picked up the internal memo, the paper crinkling loudly in the quiet office. The words detailing the sale of StellarCorp hit her like a physical blow. Her gaze darted around the open-plan workspace, cataloging the subtle shifts in body language—the way colleagues avoided eye contact, the hurried clearing of desks. She fiddled incessantly with the edge of her monitor, her fingers tracing meaningless patterns on the glass. A tightness constricted her chest, making it hard to draw a full breath. She felt intensely exposed, as if every minor flaw in her presentation was about to be scrutinized by unseen buyers.

[story 3]

I spent the entire afternoon staring at my keyboard, the blinking cursor mocking my inertia. The news had landed like a dropped weight; the entire company, the place where I felt most competent, was being bought out. I kept catching reflections in the darkened screen of my monitor, seeing only my own slightly flushed face, my hands twisting into knots beneath the desk. Every email I drafted felt clumsy, every casual interaction an act of performance. When my manager approached, offering a tentative smile, I managed a nod that felt brittle, my voice sounding strangely thin. I kept my shoulders hunched inward, trying to shrink my frame, desperate to become invisible amidst the impending corporate upheaval.

He was supposed to be finalizing the Q3 projections, but his eyes kept drifting to the sleek, unfamiliar branding plastered on the wall near the executive elevator. Sarah picked up the internal memo, the paper crinkling loudly in the quiet office. The words detailing the sale of StellarCorp hit her like a physical blow. I spent the entire afternoon staring at my keyboard, the blinking cursor mocking my inertia. The news had landed like a dropped weight; the entire company, the place where I felt most competent, was being bought out. I kept catching reflections in the darkened screen of my monitor, seeing only my own slightly flushed face, my hands twisting into knots beneath the desk.
